description: Open interest dynamics as positioning flow signal
signal_class_brief:
Open interest (OI) measures the total outstanding derivative contracts on BTC/USDT perpetual futures. Unlike volume (which measures turnover), OI tracks net positioning — whether new contracts are being created or old ones closed.

Rising OI with rising price: new longs entering, market getting more leveraged and crowded. This is structurally fragile — cascading liquidations become more likely.

Rising OI with falling price: new shorts building. Bearish positioning that creates short squeeze risk if price reverses.

Falling OI with price movement: positions being closed (deleveraging). This is often the healthiest type of move — less leverage means less liquidation risk.

The mechanism is strongly reducible: leverage creates real liquidation pressure independent of chart-watching behaviour. When OI is extreme, the market is structurally fragile regardless of what any indicator says.

In the df, the `open_interest` column contains hourly OI snapshots in quote currency (USD). Typical values are in the billions. The raw level is less meaningful than the rate of change and percentile rank — OI trends upward structurally over time as the market grows.
reducibility_note: Strong reducibility. Leverage is a structural property — high OI means high aggregate leverage, which creates liquidation cascades regardless of participant beliefs. OI dynamics capture positioning flow that is invisible in price data alone.
exploration_hints: OI rate of change: `oi_roc = open_interest.pct_change(N)` where N=24 (daily) or N=168 (weekly). Rising OI = increasing leverage.
OI percentile rank: `oi_pct = open_interest.rolling(720).rank(pct=True)` — extreme OI (>0.9) signals crowded positioning.
OI-price divergence: price rising but OI falling = short covering (healthier rally). `div = close.pct_change(24) - oi_roc`.
OI acceleration: `oi_accel = oi_roc.diff(24)` — second derivative. Positive acceleration = positioning building faster.
OI detrend: since OI grows structurally, use rolling z-score: `(oi - oi.rolling(N).mean()) / oi.rolling(N).std()`.
OI as exit signal: enter on momentum, exit when OI percentile > 0.9 (market too leveraged for the move to continue).
OI deleveraging entry: enter long when OI drops sharply (pct_change(24) < -0.05) AND price holds — the flush is over, remaining positions are stronger.
primary_signal_requirement: Your PRIMARY entry/exit logic MUST derive from the open_interest column (rate of change, percentile rank, z-score, or divergence with price). A simple trend filter (close > SMA) may be a secondary gate, but OI dynamics must be the core decision variable.
forbidden_entry_patterns: EMA or SMA crossover as the primary entry logic
Any entry condition that would be identical if the open_interest column were removed
Price z-score or momentum as the sole entry with OI as a trivial boolean gate
exploration_phase_instructions: Iteration 1: Pure OI signal. Enter when OI percentile rank (30-day rolling) drops below 0.3 (deleveraged), exit when above 0.8 (crowded). No price filters. Iteration 2: OI rate of change — enter when oi_roc(24h) < -0.03 (sharp deleveraging), exit when oi_roc(24h) > 0.05 (rapid leverage build). Iteration 3: OI-price divergence — enter when price is rising but OI is falling (healthy rally). Iteration 4: OI acceleration as entry timing. Iteration 5: Add one trend filter to the best variant.
